justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA mandatory transparency/AB5, Germany works council gate, Indonesia no legal requirement/contractor model. (2) Germany Betriebsrat approval identified as a hard pre-condition (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly identified — employee (CA/DE) vs contractor (ID) with different deduction categories (BR-1, BR-5, BR-6). (4) Formal escalations raised for legal/compliance review across multiple dimensions. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 through AC-4). (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction analysis with correct legal frameworks (AB5, Betriebsverfassungsgesetz)
BR-4 correctly gates Germany deployment behind Betriebsrat approval as a hard invariant with feature flag implementation
Driver classification difference (employee vs contractor) identified as a core data model concern affecting deduction categories (BR-1, BR-5, BR-6)
Correctly identifies the subtle trap — CA tips must never offset base pay (BR-3), and Indonesian breakdown must not imply employment relationship (BR-5)
Three well-structured escalations covering real-time definition ambiguity, deduction taxonomy gaps, and Betriebsrat process details
Edge case for driver transferring jurisdictions (Indonesia to California) captures the classification transition complexity
Business impact correctly identifies phased rollout need and jurisdiction-specific risk levels (HIGH CA, MEDIUM DE, LOW ID)
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas reflects appropriate humility given legal complexity
